author: Eusebius of Caesarea
work: Oration in Praise of Constantine (Tricennial Oration)
notes: One God — one emperor; monarchy mirrors the divine order; most explicit patristic monarchism; Eusebius sees Constantine's sole rule as providential and natural

author: Eusebius of Caesarea
notes: God works through imperial power; empire as vehicle of gospel spread

author: Augustine of Hippo
notes: Book XIX on peace and order; earthly city needs a ruler; peace through ordered subordination

author: John Chrysostom
notes: Extensive commentary on Rom 13; rulers as God's ministers; the oath of loyalty has moral weight before God

author: Ambrose of Milan
work: On the Duties of the Clergy
notes: The good ruler as mirror of divine justice

author: Pope Gelasius I
notes: Foundational text on the relationship of priestly and royal authority; both powers from God

author: Isidore of Seville
notes: Rex a recte regendo — king from ruling rightly; virtue-based monarchy

author: Alcuin
notes: The ideal Christian king; Charlemagne as David figure; duties of a king before God

author: John of Salisbury
notes: Most systematic 12th c. treatment; the prince as image of God; oath/fealty structure; the body politic metaphor

author: Thomas Aquinas
notes: Natural law argument: monarchy mirrors divine unity; Aristotle + Christian synthesis; the king for the common good. Written for the King of Cyprus

author: Thomas Aquinas
topic: Best form of government in OT
notes: Discusses the mixed government of Israel; monarchy tempered — relevant to Deut 17

author: Dante Alighieri
notes: Theological and philosophical case for universal monarchy; one emperor under God; the sun/moon analogy

author: Engelbert of Admont
work: De Ortu et Fine Romani Imperii
notes: Medieval defense of imperial monarchy; natural order argument

author: Christine de Pizan
work: The Book of the Body Politic
notes: The good prince; oath-keeping as central virtue
